topic: A neighbor wants to install a fence
raw_output:
[story 1]

The sound of the hammer striking the post felt like a physical assault on the quiet of the afternoon. I sat on the porch steps, my gaze fixed on the freshly driven metal, the glare reflecting dully in my eyes. Every movement felt monumental, a heavy lift against an invisible weight pressing down on my chest. Mrs. Henderson was humming cheerfully over the fence, a bright, relentless sound that grated against my frayed nerves. I just stared at the weeds growing stubbornly along the sidewalk, tracing the patterns in the cracked concrete with my fingertip, wishing the ground would swallow me whole.

[story 2]

"Just a little longer, Mr. Davies," she chirped, adjusting the wire. Her voice was too light, too buoyant for the gray afternoon. I nodded, a jerky, involuntary motion. My shoulders slumped further as I watched the taut line of the fence rise. It seemed impossible to summon the energy to even stand up straight. I shuffled inside, the silence of my own house amplifying the hollow echo in my ears. I just wanted the noise to stop, the pressure to ease, but all I could manage was a slow, heavy sigh that seemed to drain the last of my reserves.

[story 3]

I watched the lumber pile, stacked neatly by the curb, a monument to impending change. Each plank seemed to represent another barrier, another demarcation line between the world I was trapped in and the world that kept moving forward. My hands were cold, clammy, and I kept rubbing the back of my neck, a repetitive, anxious gesture. When the neighbor finally called out, asking if I could help secure the bottom rail, my response was a barely audible murmur. "Maybe later," I managed, the words tasting like ash in my mouth. I didn't want to help; I just wanted the whole situation to vanish.
